text:
Admission Date:  2000-08-17              Discharge Date:   1970-02-14

Date of Birth:  2010-07-27             Sex:   M

Service: ORTHOPAEDICS

Allergies:
Penicillins / Lipitor

Attending:Tamara
Chief Complaint:
72M s/p L TKA revision with left knee weakness and drainage

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
03-31: I&D and vac @ 125, retained implant
12-26: I&D and VAC at 125, retained implant
12-16: St. Mary's Medical Center to place IVC filter
09-13: I&D, Vac at 125, retained implant
02-09: I&D, Liner exchange, medial gastroc flap, skin graft


History of Present Illness:
This is a 72 year old male with a medical history significant
for
DM2, HTN, HLD who recently (1985-10-22) underwent a complex
revision
left total knee replacement (with hinged knee prosthesis) for a
failed primary left total knee arthroplasty. He was admitted on
06-26 for a failed total knee replacement and underwent a planned
revision left total knee replacement which was converted to a
resection arthroplasty of all components with extensive
synovectomy.  His course was complicated by hypotension related
to blood loss from first surgery which required an ICU stay and
small PE diagnosed for which he was started on coumarin. He was
then taken back to the OR on 01-20 for the complex revision of
the
left TKR as above.  He was monitored in the ICU after that
operation for unclear reasons.  He was discharged in a stable
condition on his home medications.

He reports that at the time of discharge he was having a lot of
pain and some serosanguineous drainage from his knee.  He was
initially able to walk with a walker but over the course of the
weeks he was slowly unable to walk.  About 1 week prior to admit
he developed fevers/chills and some redness and swelling of the
knee.  The swelling increased to the point where he could not
move the knee at all.  That is what ultimately prompted him to
come to the ED.

He then presented to the ED with bloody drainage from his left
knee weakness and chills.  An XR of the knee on 12-16 revealed
some
gas in the subcutaneous tissue and joint space which was
concerning for an infection.  His WBC count was initially normal
but increased to 17 on 03-31.  An ESR was elevated at 95 and a CRP
was 200.1.  The knee was tapped on 03-20 and the cell count
revealed 3d337051-b370-4de8-88ff-63c65650170a WBC (95% polys).  The gram stain revealed 1+
polys.  Of
note his Cr was also elevated from last admit at 1.4 up from
baseline of 1.


Past Medical History:
T2DM
HTN
Hepatitis B (carrier)
Hyperlipidemia
Gout
Nephrolithiasis
Osteoarthritis (R hip)
Allergic rhinitis
Sciatica
L knee replacement 1988
s/p screw removal in L femur before 2007.


Social History:
The patient grew up in 255 Kristin Ramp
Lake Michael, SD 83423-2310, now lives in PSC 5383, Box 7601
APO AP 35873 with his

life partner. Carl Day quit smoking 30 yrs ago, denies EtOH or
recreational drug use. He used to be a hairdresser, retired
four years ago.


Family History:
Father died at 55 of an MI. Mother died of breast CA at 56.
Sister has diabetes.

Physical Exam:
Well appearing in no acute distress
Afebrile with stable vital signs
Pain well-controlled

Respiratory: CTAB
Cardiovascular: RRR
Gastrointestinal: NT/ND
Genitourinary: Voiding independently

Neurologic: Intact with no focal deficits
Psychiatric: Pleasant, A&O x3

Musculoskeletal Lower Extremity:
5/5 strength TA/GS/Hernandez/FHL
SILT DP/SP/T/S/S
Toes warm
incision: dressed with xeroform and light dressing covered with
ace wrap and knee immobilizer.


Pertinent Results:

BASOS-0.3

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the orthopaedic surgery service on
2000-08-17 with the complaint of meft knee weakness and drainage.
He noted that while at rehab he became progressively unable to
extend his knee and noticed reddness and dranage along the
inferior aspect of his incision.  The diagnosis of a patellar
tendon rupture was made after admission.  There was an area of
erythema located over the distal end of his incision that became
progressive over his first 2 days of admission.  He Clark to
spike fevers and subsequently had his knee tapped which revealed
13,000WBC and a culture that eventually became positve for MSSA.
 He also had a fever spike as high as 104.3 and had a positive
blood culture for MSSA.  He was taken to the operating room on
1985-02-05 for a left knee I&D and a VAC dressing placement.  He
was once again taken to the OR on 1984-04-16 for repeat I+D and VAC
change.  All implats were retained during these procedures.  He
was left intubated following his second procedure for
intermittnet hypotension and respiratory distress.  He was
extubated in the ICU without issue.  He received an IVC filter
on 1993-09-09 and was taken back to the OR for a repeat I+D and
VAC change on 1984-11-02 with retaiend hardware.  He was taken
back to the OR for definitive repair on 1993-12-14 in combination
with Plastic surgery for a repeat I+D, liner exchange, medial
gastrocnemius flap and split thicknedd skin grafting.  He had a
VAC drain placed as a bolster and [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Fuentes drain in place.  He
was kept on bedrest for 5 days following this procedure.  He had
a short stay in the ICU for monitoring but was subsequently
transferred to the floor in stable condition following this
procedure. Please see separately dictated operative reports for
details.  The patient initially received Vancomycin until
culture speciation and was subsequently trnasitioned to Ancef
and Rifampin per Infectios Disease who were consulted regarding
his care.  The patient received several traunsfusions of PRBC
and FFP for falling hematocrit and INR reversal respectively.
He was anticoagulated on therapeutic Lovenox during his stay for
known prior pulmonary embolus.

Otherwise, pain was initially controlled with a PCA followed by
a transition to oral pain medications on POD#1. At the time of
discharge the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling
well.  The patient was afebrile with stable vital signs.  The
patient's hematocrit was acceptable and pain was adequately
controlled on an oral regimen. The operative extremity was
neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign.

At time of discharge, patient was deemed stable for safe
discharge to rehab.

The patient's weight-bearing status is touch down weight bearing
as tolerated on the operative extremity locked in extension.

9. Bisacodyl 10 mg Suppository Sig: One (1) Suppository Rectal

10. insulin sliding scale  Sig: One (1)   daily: as per

11. Rifampin 300 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO Q12H (every

12. Cefazolin 10 gram Recon Soln Sig: One (1) Recon Soln
Injection Q8H (every 8 hours): 2 g every 8 hours.
13. picc line  Sig: One (1)   once a day: picc line care as per
protocol.
14. Heparin, Porcine (PF) 10 unit/mL Syringe Sig: One (1) ML
Intravenous PRN (as needed) as needed for line flush.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Roane General Hospital Center - Gothenburg Memorial Hospital

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left complex revision TKA infection/patellar tendon avulsion


Discharge Condition:
Stable, TDWB LLE, Knee immobilzer locked in extension x 6 weeks


Discharge Instructions:
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5,
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this
admission and any new medications and refills.
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily
and take a stool
softener (such as colace) as needed to prevent this side effect.
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your
surgeon or your primary physician.
6. Please keep your wounds clean. daily dressing changes should
be performed as directed. Xeroform covering over the incision as
well as the graft site. An ace bandage should also be applied.
7. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your
follow-up appointment in four weeks. You need to be seen in 2
weeks by your plastic surgeon.
8. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory
medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve,
motrin, etc).
9. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your lovenox for three weeks
to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).
10. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean. Xeroform over
incision and ace bandage
11. VNA (once at home): Home PT/OT, dressing changes as
instructed, wound checks, and staple removal at two weeks after
surgery.
12. ACTIVITY: NON WEIGHT BEARING> DANGLE PROTOCOL UNTIL SEEN BY
PLASTIC SURGEON***5-10 minutes three times a day only. other
wise knee immobilizer in place full time. NO FLEXION No
strenuous exercise or heavy lifting until follow up appointment.

Physical Therapy:
Knee immobilizer in extension x 6 weeks
Dangle protocol 5-10 min three times a day only until follow up
otherwise bed rest and sitting in chair with leg elevated
Treatments Frequency:
-Please place xeroform dressing to skin graft sight (upper left
thigh) and down medial incision (interior of calf) once daily.
-Please do wet to dry dressing to open area at bottom portion of
medial calf incision once daily.
-cover all incisions and graft site with kerlix.
-Wrap with ACE from left foot up to top of suture line and
re-apply knee immobilizer.
-Elevate LLE as much as possible.
